sentences.text: So, as I said, things from the very beginning were already-- a picture was beginning to form itself of what it was-- what we were to expect.
But, nonetheless, there we were, all very apprehensive.
Slowly, slowly they started taking people to work.
What should I say?
Beating people up.
When I say people I now mean the Jewish population.

You see, we were in a very-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] very unhappy position in Lwow particularly and in that part of Poland because we had-- we were surrounded by the Germans, by the Ukrainians, and by the Polish population locally.
The Germans without the help of the local population would not have been able to achieve what they achieved, not by half.
And I may be optimistic when I say that.
They could never have aided know who was, who was not Jewish.
There were no lists.
There were no-- secondly, the looks alone-- no.
That didn't make the slightest difference, unless you had such a prominent Semitic aspect.
But otherwise they wouldn't have known.
There were assisted enormously.
At the same time in all honesty, and it's absolutely a fact that some helped the Germans, others helped us.
There were those and there were those.

Those who helped the people to survive, or kept them in hiding, deserve every accolade under the sun, if there is such an accolade at all because they all, every second of their lives, could pay with their lives.
Not just their own, but their nearest also.
The families could go with them.
So what ever they did, there is no gratitude that's enough.
Right?
On the other hand, the others who, just for the fun of it, would denounce.
Some denounced because they had material gain.
Some denounced for reasons of, I don't know.
Vengeance, or God knows.
But, on the whole, they denounced for fun.
Just like that.
It was so easy to say, here, here.
This is a Jew.
That was all.
That's all that was needed.
And they didn't need any qualifications or any explanations on the part of such a person who was called.
That was it.
It was enough.
To have been a Jew was quite adequate that you are a victim.
Yes.
And there we were exposed to that all the time.
Selections began.
Slowly, slowly.
Mounting.
And they would take men away.
They would take old people away.
They would take sick people away.
They would take-- they would take God knows why somebody away.
For no reason.
Just like that.
Selections.
The ghetto was nonexistent as yet.
We were still living wherever.
But slowly, slowly they started shifting the Jewish population in one direction, behind the bridges, behind the embankment, the railway embankment, and building a ghetto there for them.
Not building.
I mean, putting them up in the houses over there.
And I now skip a little bit because it's no good me telling you in absolute minutest detail, but I will go to the moment where my father was taken.
My parents were-- sorry.
All of us were taken away a number of times.
We were in a selection.
And somehow, by a miracle, God knows how, we managed to-- my mother would always let us go, the children, and somehow we-- I took the little boy.
We managed to escape.
And then from the outside we tried to see that somehow or other, one can get our parents out.
And on two such occasions it happened and there they were and we were all together again, and all right.
I'm telling you this very briefly because this is impossible to tell you what it was really like.
I mean, to give you a picture of that is very difficult.
But it happened.
Because it's difficult to envisage, envisage for anybody who was not part of it, what it was like.
To put this into words is an impossibility.
When you are standing in a group of people and one tells-- and there is an SS man says, left, right, left, right, and you don't know who is going left and you are going left.
Your mother is going right.
Your father is going right.
Your mother, brother is going left.
You don't know.
Are they going-- why?
Are they going to die?
Are we going to live?
Is it wise-- it's very difficult to put this into words.
And there were moments where it was pure instinct.
You just worked on pure instinct.
No.
I'm not staying in this.
I'm going on to that.
And sometimes it was just a wrong decision.
But sometimes it was the right decision.
So it's difficult to depict.
But this is the usual, the everyday events that took place.
But in August, 1942, they began to talk about a very specific selection, a selection which will take children away.
Nobody wanted to believe that.
Children?
Nonsense.
Who is going to take children?
What do they want children for?
And what are they going to do with children?
No, no, no.
That's not right.
Nobody wanted to believe.
When my father said it seems, I don't know.
I think they're capable of everything.
I think it's very possible that they will.
In this case, he wanted-- women and children.
Women and children.
He wanted my mother and me out of the house and into hiding.
And he said-- he has a very good document working where he does, where he was working, again, as a carpenter and cabinet maker.
He worked for a German authority this time.
And he said my papers are very adequate because this is-- he worked for what they called the [NON-ENGLISH], which was, you know, quite an important establishment.
And he said nothing is going to happen to me and I stay with the boy.
Child will go to work with me and so on.
You go into hiding.
My mother didn't want to.
Eventually she condescended and she took me and we went.
Yes.
My mother wanted my brother to come.
But the people where we were going to be hidden, it was next house to where we live, the very next house, there was a shop, and milliners shop.
And there were two sisters, two Jewish ladies, who were running the shop.

And they said to my mother that they are going to close the shop up and use it as a hiding place, which is absolutely-- when you think about it now, or even later when I thought about, when one thought about it, it was ludicrous because what sort of a hiding place was that?
But in once naive state of mind in those days, at the beginning, one thought this was-- shop is closed so there is nobody there.
So my mother said, all right.
May I please come with the two children?
He said, no, and they said, no way, and certainly not the little boy.
He's too young.
He may cry.
Something may happen.
I don't know.
They didn't want it.
So my father said, this is not-- no problem.
He will be with me.
And I'm covered.
My papers cover me absolutely, 100%.
And so we stay and you go there with lady, with me.
And we did, my mother very reluctantly.
We did.
And they had an arrangement that every day when my father would go to work with the boy and when they were coming back, they would pass by this shop and we'd be by the keyhole and we'd see them.
And so we know, more or less, all is well.
And so it was for two days.
And then the third day we didn't see them pass.
And then suddenly two women coming up, leaning against the-- whatever.
Crate, or what was against the door of the shop.
And talking to one another and saying these words.
Well, you know, from our house they took two away.
Oh, yes.
Who did they take?
So she said, well, you know.
This nice Mr.
Stern with his little boy.
My mother and I, we were like made of ice, of stone, of concrete.
We were completely rigid.
Nothing.
No emotion.
The two ladies with us screamed out and one of them fainted.
And then, as if it hadn't been enough, as if maybe we didn't understand it, they repeated the whole thing all over again.
She said, one said, oh, you don't-- who do you mean?
How did-- what?
No.
It can't be.
So she repeated, oh, yes.
You know?
This little, lovely boy and the father.
And this is how we learned they took my father away with the boy.
Do you think that they had deliberately said it knowing that you were there?
No.
Not at all.
Just a complete coincidence.
It was an absolute coincidence.
One was from our house and the other one was from this house.
They were-- one was a caretaker of this particular house.
From that house, I don't even know who it was anymore.
But they were just standing and discussing and talking about it.
And [DRINKS] must've been about 5:00, 6:00 o'clock in the afternoon.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] And my mother said, I'm going out.
I'm going out to find them.
And these women said, no, no, no.
You must not.
Of course, why, dear, you can't go out of here.
And why they, of course, it was right.
One shouldn't.
So we waited until the evening came, and that was a few hours because it was August.
And when it was dark, she slid out and went first of all to his work, where my father was working.
And there was one officer there whose name I remember very vividly until today who was sort of particularly nice to my father.
Again, he admired a lot his work.
He always came to look how he's working.
And he said to him, you know, no way are you a carpenter.
And my father would say, what do you mean?
I mean, would I be a carpenter here if I were not a-- why do you say that?
He said, no, no.
I've never seen a carpenter work the way you do.
No, no, no.
Not that way.
You're not a carpenter, but nevermind.
But nevermind.
You do a beautiful job.
You stay.
He would occasionally pop in a chocolate into his pocket for the children.
So he had some human aspect in him.
There was something, although he was in a [NON-ENGLISH] uniform and so forth.
But he was a little bit more human than some of the others.
And so my mother decide, she went straight to him and he said, yes.
I don't understand what happened.
He didn't come to work today.
So my mother said, look, I-- they were taken.
He said, what?
Taken away?
By whom?
They were the people who very often gathered them, you know.
By whom?
She said, I don't know by whom.
I wasn't there.
But they were taken away.
We have to find them.
Would you please come and help me to find them?
He took her in the car.
They went all over the town, into every possible niche where there could have been people taken from selections, here and there.
This was like-- he just disappeared.
Both of them disappeared off the face of this Earth.
Nobody knew what happened to him-- how, where, why.
Which way, who took them, so on.
Nobody knew.
And he, this officer, was so adamant in finding because his pride was, you know, what?
My man?
He was my man!
He had my paper in his hand.
He was an untouchable person.
Nobody was allowed to touch him.
And yet.
How?
Who dared?
So that was a point of ambition and pride to find such a-- but no.
Even he couldn't find.

So the following day my mother went to our caretaker and when she opened the door, he stood there dressed top to bottom in my father's clothes, wearing my father's watch, and the radio from our home was playing there.
And as he was sort of the similar stature and dark hair and so on, at first she thought it was my father.
You know?
And she actually shouted, and he turned around and there he was.
Now, this man was a Ukrainian militia.
When the Russians came first, he worked for the Russians.
When the Germans came immediately, his little, you know, thing-- the wind blew the other way and he immediately became a militiaman.
And, of course, that was very-- not very pleasant to have such a person around.
But there he was and he was our caretaker, the house caretaker.
Often he would come and talk to my father.
He liked him, so to speak.
Talked to him.
And during the course of the conversation, he would say, oh, you know, Mr.
Stern, I like your watch.
It's a very nice watch.
I like it.
So my father said, thank you.
I like it too.
Oh, I wouldn't mind having a watch like this.
In other words, take it off and give it to me.
But, unfortunately, my father was not that way inclined.
He was not one to bribe.
He was not one-- he was completely different sort.
He was a very upright and very honest and very proud person.
He would not-- and my mother said several time, give it to him.
Give him something.
No.
No.
This is not a way.
I'm not giving.
Why?
This was a present from you.
I'm not giving it to anyone.
Then he would come say, you know, your radio.
It plays so well.
Mine is very bad.
I can't play on my radio.
I like your radio very much.
And so it went on.
And he was the one to bring whoever-- nobody could ever get us-- two Gestapos, and he-- and they took him away.
Apparently.
Apparently.
That's hearsay, but-- excuse me.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] One of the Gestapo men wanted to take the boy aside and tell him to go, but he didn't want to leave.
He didn't want to.
Wanted to stay with the father.
And that was that.
Now-- [DRINKS] the 15th of August 1942 is when my father and my brother, as far as we're concerned, died.
Died by whose hand, by what means, we shall never know.
From that moment on when we stayed behind, I need not go into-- needn't tell you how broken my mother was to lose a husband and a child in one day.
And so she had me, but I was no substitute.
But I was there and she had to fend for me.
And in those days although I was young-- I was a child, really, but one was not a child anymore.
One was very, very what you would call today streetwise, I imagine.
One was much more than streetwise.
One was certainly-- when I think of things today, I don't think I could do today what I could do then being aged 10, 11, 12, 13.
Wouldn't occur to me that it might be possible, but one did then.
And so my mother and I went to our grandparents and stayed with them.
And then we had to slowly move into the ghetto.
They were now moving everybody into the ghetto.
And the ghetto was not closed up yet completely.
It was, in other words, what they called an open ghetto.
That meant that people could go out to work out of the ghetto.
Because there was no work in the ghetto they had-- all the labor force was from the ghetto to the outside, to the other side.
So it was open.
And we lived then with my grandparents inside the ghetto for a few months.
Selections, no selections.
All these things were happening.
The ghetto was, of course, thriving.
People were living-- [COUGHS] excuse me-- in appalling conditions.
How many to one room I will not specify.
It was just almost subhuman.
But there were no other-- there was no other way.
And there we were.
And my mother was working at that time for an institution called the [NON-ENGLISH], which is-- what can I say?
It's like-- it's social services.
Accommodation department, right?
They were accommodating people.
They were assessing who deserves what apartment and so on.
It was called [NON-ENGLISH] and it was a civilian, German institution.
Not military.
Civilian.
Well, she-- how, I cannot tell you.
But everybody had to work.
And so she-- one recommended another, and she was maybe not even by recommendation, but whoever needed.
There were always notices put out.
So and such and such labor is required by so-and-so.
Such and such labor by so-and-so.
And so people would go, voluntarily, and offer their services.
In other words, I am a dressmaker.
Right.
You go and work here.
This is how.
And she was put into that particular institution and working there.
And she was-- when I say a dressmaker, she was far more than the dresser.
She was truly a fashion design.
She was fashion creator before the war.
But with very elegant clientele and so forth.
But here-- but what she could do, again-- her hands they called golden hands because she did beautiful job.
Whatever she finished was something unusual.
She never did frilly or fancy models or anything like-- no.
Her style was always very elegant, simple, sporty.
And people liked that, particularly those ladies whom she was dressing.
And there she was dressing some of the top secretaries in the institution, one of whom was a very unusual looking person in as much that she was very tall, very slim, very tall, and very shy.
She was shy probably because she was so tall.
She always used to say that.
That she feels that everybody always looks at her because they see her in front of everyone else.
She's striking.
And she didn't like that.
And consequently she liked to have very simple clothes.
She wore very simple.
She wore blouses, skirts.
Blouses, skirts.
But, of course, here was an opportunity to her, not two or three blouses.
To have 25 blouses to change and 25 skirts.
You see?
And my mother-- and she adored my mother solely.
Whatever my mother said was holy word for her.
And she was sort of number one whom my mother was dressing.
When the boy-- when my father and the boy were taken away, my mother was sitting there working, crying all the time.
She would come in.
Why are you crying?
What's the matter with you?
Always crying.
You're never smiling.
And she was, of course, a German with the occupied forces.
My mother said, well, I'm sorry if I cry.
I'm sorry if it distresses you, but I can't help it because I've lost my husband.
I've lost my child.
What do you mean, you've lost your child?
What do you mean you lost your husband?
She couldn't understand that at all.
She couldn't understand, the meaning of it.
So my mother would tell her and she-- how could you-- how could you allow this to happen?
What do you mean allow?
And so, you know, it was talking cross-purposes.
This woman was totally unaware of what was going on.
Totally unaware.
Even though she's around surrounded by Jews working there, so many and so on, she was unaware.
And she said-- and she kept looking.
My mother had a photograph of my father and the boy and so on.
And so she was coming and looking at this one and she said, I can't understand.
I can't understand.
Such a boy.
How could you allow it?
Occasionally, you know, they talked like this.
But on the whole, it was just a job done and that's it and she would go back to the ghetto.
On one such day when she was going to go out to work she said to us that morning I won't be coming home this evening.

So don't worry about me because I was asked to stay the night with so-and-so and she told us the name, three sisters, who've been begging me since weeks and weeks to stay and do something for them, to sew something-- you know, dressmaking.
And although she's been telling them how dangerous it is and it is, of course, the penalty is death for everybody.
But they said, oh, no.
Nobody will see you if you come at night.
Nobody will see you.
Do come and stay one night.
